narrative.intro: Geneva is small, walkable, and stitched together by one of the world's most efficient transit systems. Geneva Airport (GVA) sits 4 km from the centre and is connected by a 7-minute train ride. Here's what actually works.

arrival.title: From the Airport
arrival.content:
**GVA → city:** Pick up the FREE 80-minute **Unireso transit ticket** from the red machine in baggage claim (just before customs) — then take the **Léman Express / CFF train** to Gare de Genève-Cornavin. **7 minutes, every 10–15 min.** It's the fastest airport-to-centre link in Europe.

**Buses:** TPG bus 5 to Pâquis / UN quarter, TPG bus 10 to Rive / Old Town. Both also free with the airport voucher.

**Taxis:** Metered, expensive. Expect CHF 35–50 to anywhere central. Uber works (no Bolt). Use only for late flights or ski-trip groups with bulky luggage.

**Hotel guests:** Geneva hotels issue a free **Geneva Transport Card** at check-in covering local public transit for your entire stay. Always ask.

gettingAround.title: Getting Around
gettingAround.content:
**Walk first.** The Old Town, Rade (harbour), Pâquis, and Eaux-Vives are all within 15 minutes of each other on foot. Geneva is genuinely small.

**TPG trams and buses** cover everything else. Trams 12, 14, 15, 17, 18 run frequently. Tickets via app or station machines; the free Geneva Transport Card covers all of it.

**Mouettes Genevoises** (yellow water buses) cross the lake every 10 minutes between Pâquis, Eaux-Vives, and Molard — covered by the Transport Card. The most charming commute in Europe.

**Léman Express** is the regional train backbone — runs to the airport, Annemasse, Annecy, Évian, and across the Geneva canton.

**Bikes:** Genève Roule rents bikes (and free city bikes 4 hours a day in summer). The lakefront paths are perfect for cycling.

money.title: Money & Payments
money.content:
**Cards everywhere.** Visa/Mastercard tap-to-pay accepted at virtually every café, bistro, museum, and shop. Amex is patchier.

**Currency is Swiss francs (CHF), not euros.** Many places near the train station and airport accept euros at a poor rate — pay in CHF.

**Tipping:** Service is included. Round up to the nearest franc or leave 5–10% for outstanding service.

**ATMs** are plentiful — UBS and Raiffeisen offer the fairest rates. Avoid Travelex.

**Geneva is expensive.** A coffee is CHF 5, a beer CHF 8, a casual main course CHF 25–35. Budget accordingly.

connectivity.title: Staying Connected
connectivity.content:
**WiFi is universal** — hotels, cafés, museums, libraries, trams, and many public squares offer free networks.

**EU SIMs roam fine** but Switzerland is NOT in the EU — check whether your plan includes Switzerland (many do not). For longer trips, a Swisscom or Salt prepaid SIM (CHF 25–40) covers solid data.

**Outlets: Type J, 230V.** Swiss plugs are unique — bring an adapter even if you have European plugs. Most hotels stock a few at the front desk.

timing.title: Local Rhythms
timing.content:
**Shops:** Mon–Sat ~9am–7pm, Thursday late nights to 8 or 9pm. Most shops closed Sunday — bakeries and corner stores at the train station open.

**Restaurants:** Lunch 12–2pm, dinner from 7pm; many kitchens close by 10pm. Late dining is limited outside Pâquis and Carouge.

**Museums:** Most closed Mondays (MAH, Patek Philippe). Many free on the first Sunday of the month. Check.

**Public holidays:** Geneva has Jeûne Genevois (Thursday after first Sunday of September) and Restauration de la République (December 31, observed lightly). Almost everything closes on January 1, August 1 (Swiss National Day), and December 25–26.

**Cross-border:** Many day trips (Chamonix, Annecy, Yvoire) cross into France. Always carry a passport.

quickWins.title: Insider Moves
quickWins.content:
**Free transit voucher.** Pick it up at the airport before customs — saves you a taxi fare on day one.

**Free hotel transport card.** Ask at check-in. Covers all TPG and Mouettes for your stay.

**Tap water is excellent.** Ask for 'une carafe d'eau, s'il vous plaît' — free and superb.

**Greet properly.** Always start with 'bonjour' (or 'bonsoir' after 5pm). Skipping it is rude.

**Sunday means lake walks.** Most shops close — locals migrate to the quais, parks, and Bains des Pâquis.

**The bise wind makes cold feel colder.** Pack a windproof shell in autumn through spring.

faqs.question: Is Geneva safe for tourists?
faqs.answer: Yes — among the safest cities in Europe. The most common issue is petty pickpocketing around Cornavin station and the Pâquis nightlife area. The wider city, including the Old Town and lakefront, is very calm by day and night.

faqs.question: How much should I budget per day?
faqs.answer: Geneva is one of the most expensive cities in Europe. A comfortable mid-range budget is CHF 250–350 per person per day (hotel, meals, transit, attractions). Budget travelers can manage on CHF 130–180 with hostels and casual dining. Luxury easily runs CHF 600+.

faqs.question: Do I need to speak French?
faqs.answer: Geneva is the largest French-speaking Swiss city, but English is widely spoken in hotels, restaurants, museums, and shops. Learning 'bonjour,' 'merci,' 's'il vous plaît,' and 'parlez-vous anglais?' goes a long way. Most Genevans switch to English fluently when needed.

faqs.question: What's the best area to stay?
faqs.answer: Pâquis is the most practical — central, near Cornavin, with the lake at your doorstep. Eaux-Vives offers quieter lakefront luxury. Old Town gives historic charm but limited car access. Carouge is for travellers who want a residential, café-rich feel — quick tram into the centre.

faqs.question: Should I buy transit tickets?
faqs.answer: Almost never. The airport voucher covers your arrival, your hotel issues a free Geneva Transport Card covering your full stay (always ask at check-in), and the Léman Express and Mouettes are included. Only buy a ticket if you're a day-tripper without a hotel.

faqs.question: Is the tap water safe to drink?
faqs.answer: Excellent — Geneva tap water comes directly from the lake and underground sources and is among the best in Europe. Restaurants will bring a free carafe if you ask for 'une carafe d'eau.' Public fountains across the city dispense potable water.

faqs.question: How do I get to ski resorts from Geneva?
faqs.answer: Easily. Chamonix, Verbier, Les Portes du Soleil, and Megève are 60–120 minutes away. Dedicated ski shuttles (AlpyBus, Mountain Drop-Offs) collect from the airport. Trains via SBB to Martigny or Aigle for higher Swiss resorts. Avoid the city entirely on ski-day Saturdays — go airport-to-resort direct.

faqs.question: What should I definitely not miss?
faqs.answer: Beyond the obvious (Jet d'Eau, Old Town, Reformation Wall): a Mouettes water-bus crossing of the harbour at sunset, the Patek Philippe Museum, a fondue dinner in Carouge, the cable car up Mont Salève for the Mont Blanc panorama, and apéro at Bains des Pâquis any time of year.
